chapter_info.subject: Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents and Solutions

points: A solution is a uniform mixture formed when one substance (solute) completely dissolves in another (solvent), creating even distribution throughout. Non-uniform mixtures like chalk in water show visible separation of components.

In solid-liquid solutions, the solid is solute and liquid is solvent. In liquid-liquid solutions, the smaller-quantity component is solute and the larger-quantity component is solvent. Air is a gaseous solution with nitrogen as solvent and oxygen/argon/CO₂ as solutes.

An unsaturated solution can dissolve more solute at its given temperature; a saturated solution has reached maximum solute dissolution capacity and can dissolve no more at that temperature. Adding more solute to saturated solution results in undissolved solid settling at the bottom.

Solubility (maximum solute amount dissolvable in fixed solvent volume) increases with temperature for most solids but decreases for gases. A saturated solution becomes unsaturated when heated—additional solute can then dissolve. This temperature dependence is critical for many industrial and biological processes.

Density is mass per unit volume (Density = Mass/Volume) and is an intensive property independent of object size. Density depends on substance type and environmental conditions (temperature, pressure) but not on shape or amount of material.

Mass is measured using balances in grams or kilograms; volume is measured using measuring cylinders for liquids or displacement method for irregular solids. Accurate density calculation requires precise measurement of both mass and volume using appropriate laboratory instruments.

Objects float if density is less than surrounding liquid's density; objects sink if density is greater; neutral buoyancy occurs at equal density. This explains why oil floats on water, iron sinks, and ice floats—each has different density relative to water.

Temperature affects density: most substances decrease in density when heated (volume increases while mass stays constant). Water exhibits anomalous behavior: maximum density at 4°C, so ice floats on liquid water—a property essential for aquatic life survival in frozen conditions.

Pressure affects density differently by state: gases compress under pressure (density increases significantly), liquids are nearly incompressible (minimal density change), solids are extremely incompressible (negligible density change). These differences reflect how tightly particles pack in each state.

Measuring cylinders are designed narrow and tall to maximize accuracy: narrow design amplifies small volume changes into noticeable height changes on the scale. Reading the meniscus (curved liquid surface) at eye level, observing the bottom curve for clear liquids and top curve for coloured liquids ensures accurate volume measurement.
